answer:
\boxed{I}  

The attorney-client privilege generally survives the end of the representation, so you must assert the privilege on behalf of the former client unless the client has explicitly waived it, typically in writing. Providing confidential information without a valid waiver could violate ethical duties and expose you to professional discipline. A court order may eventually compel disclosure, but the privilege should be asserted until a proper waiver or judicial ruling is obtained.
